development_status: As of 2025-11-07, the ASCENT-07 primary analysis was announced, indicating that the trial did not meet its primary endpoint of BICR-assessed PFS. Overall survival data is still immature, but an early trend favoring Trodelvy was observed, and patient follow-up will continue to further assess OS. The safety profile was consistent with previous Trodelvy breast cancer studies, with no new safety signals identified. The trial remains active for OS follow-up, and Gilead plans to present the full ASCENT-07 data at an upcoming medical conference.

mechanism_context: Sacituzumab govitecan-hziy (Trodelvy) is a Trop-2–directed antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) that delivers SN-38 (the active metabolite of irinotecan, a topoisomerase I inhibitor) via a proprietary hydrolyzable linker. This ADC targets Trop-2 (a cell-surface antigen highly expressed in many tumor types) and produces activity both in Trop-2–expressing tumor cells and in the tumor microenvironment through a bystander effect. Trop-2 is highly expressed in >90% of breast and lung cancers, and the SN-38 payload is a topoisomerase I inhibitor.

progress_timeline_date: On 2025-11-07, Gilead announced that the ASCENT-07 study did not meet its primary endpoint of progression-free survival (PFS) at the primary analysis. The trial enrolled 654 patients across nearly 30 countries. Overall survival (OS), a key secondary endpoint, was not mature at the time of the primary analysis, but an early trend favoring Trodelvy was observed, and follow-up will continue to assess OS. The trial was initiated and enrolled between approximately 2023 and 2025, with abstracts/presentations in 2023/2024 and the primary analysis announced in late 2025. Exact registry start and primary/estimated completion dates were not provided in the press release or abstract, but can be found on ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT05840211).

trial_design: ASCENT-07 is a Phase 3, randomized, open-label, global study. Patients were randomized 2:1 to receive either sacituzumab govitecan-hziy (10 mg/kg IV on Days 1 and 8 of a 21-day cycle) or treatment of physician’s choice (TPC) single-agent chemotherapy (capecitabine, paclitaxel, or nab-paclitaxel). Treatment continued until BICR-verified progression or unacceptable toxicity. The patient population included adults (≥18 years) with histologically confirmed HR+/HER2-negative (HER2 IHC 0 or HER2-low [IHC1+, IHC2+/ISH–]), locally advanced inoperable or metastatic disease, previously treated with endocrine therapy and eligible for first-line cytotoxic chemotherapy, with measurable disease per RECIST v1.1 and ECOG 0–1. Key exclusion criteria included prior treatment targeting topoisomerase I. The study enrolled approximately 654 patients. The primary endpoint was progression-free survival (PFS) assessed by Blinded Independent Central Review (BICR) per RECIST v1.1. Key secondary endpoints included overall survival (OS), objective response rate (ORR), quality of life (QoL; EORTC QLQ‑C30 domains), safety, pharmacokinetics, and immunogenicity.

mechanism_context: Giredestrant is an investigational, oral, next-generation selective oestrogen receptor degrader (SERD) and full antagonist. It is designed to block oestrogen from binding the oestrogen receptor, triggering receptor breakdown (degradation) and thereby stopping or slowing cancer cell growth. Targeting ER in ER-positive breast cancer aims to prevent oestrogen-driven tumour proliferation and overcome endocrine resistance; SERDs both antagonize and degrade ER protein, which can be advantageous versus therapies that only block signalling.

trial_design: Trial: lidERA Breast Cancer (ClinicalTrials.gov NCT04961996). Phase: Phase III, randomized, open-label, multicentre, global study. Arms: Experimental arm (Arm A): Giredestrant 30 mg orally once daily (Days 1–28 of each 28-day cycle) for up to 5 years or until disease recurrence or unacceptable toxicity. LHRH agonist is used for male and pre-/perimenopausal participants as appropriate. Active comparator (Arm B): Endocrine therapy of physician’s choice (standard-of-care endocrine monotherapy). Patient population: Participants with histologically confirmed ER-positive, HER2-negative early breast cancer (stage I–III) at medium or high risk of recurrence. An exploratory open-label substudy evaluates giredestrant + abemaciclib in a subset. Randomization & masking: Randomized, parallel assignment, open-label (no masking). Primary endpoint: Invasive disease-free survival (iDFS) excluding unrelated cancers in other organs (i.e., excluding second primary non-breast cancers). Key secondary endpoints: Overall survival (OS), iDFS including second primary non-breast cancers, disease-free survival, and safety. Enrollment: Roche reports over 4,100 patients enrolled in lidERA.

description: sacituzumab tirumotecan (MK‑2870) is an investigational TROP2‑directed antibody‑drug conjugate being evaluated in a Phase 3 trial (NCT06074588) for non‑small cell lung cancer. The trial tests the drug alone or in combination with pembrolizumab (Keytruda) as a first‑line therapy for patients with PD‑L1‑positive disease. It is sponsored by Merck in collaboration with Sichuan Kelun‑Biotech and has received regulatory attention as a breakthrough candidate.

mechanism_context: Sacituzumab tirumotecan (sac-TMT; MK-2870) is an investigational antibody-drug conjugate that uses a TROP2-targeting monoclonal antibody (sacituzumab) to bind TROP2-expressing tumor cells. It carries a cytotoxic payload from the topoisomerase-1 inhibitor class linked via a novel, irreversible but hydrolyzable linker, with an average drug-to-antibody ratio of approximately 7.4. This TROP2 ADC strategy concentrates TOPO1-inhibitor cytotoxicity in epithelial tumors that overexpress TROP2.
mechanism_context_basis: {"field":"mechanism_context","reasoning":"The reasoning for the mechanism of action is derived from the analysis of clinical trial records and sponsor materials, which consistently describe sacituzumab tirumotecan (sac-TMT; MK-2870) as a TROP2-directed antibody-drug conjugate (ADC). This ADC delivers a topoisomerase-1 class cytotoxic payload via a proprietary linker, with a high drug-to-antibody ratio (~7.4). target_population: Adults (≥18 years) with advanced or metastatic non‑squamous NSCLC harboring EGFR exon 19del/L858R or other specified genomic alterations (ALK, ROS1, BRAF V600E, NTRK fusions, MET exon 14 skipping, RET, less common EGFR mutations). Must have progressed after 1‑2 prior EGFR‑TKI lines (including third‑generation if T790M) and after one platinum‑based regimen. Measurable disease per RECIST 1.1, tumor tissue required, stable brain metastases allowed, active CNS disease excluded.

mechanism_of_action: Sacituzumab tirumotecan (MK‑2870) is a TROP2‑directed antibody‑drug conjugate composed of a humanized anti‑TROP2 antibody linked via a sulfonyl‑pyrimidine carbonate linker to the topoisomerase I inhibitor payload KL610023. The linker undergoes irreversible nucleophilic aromatic substitution, providing stability and delivering the cytotoxic payload intracellularly to induce DNA damage in TROP2‑expressing tumor cells.

development_status: Taletrectinib is in active clinical development. It has prior clinical data from Phase I and regional/global Phase II trials demonstrating activity and tolerability in ROS1-positive NSCLC. An expanded access program (NCT06841874) also exists. The current Phase 3 adjuvant trial (NCT07154706) has an actual start date in August 2025, indicating its progression to registrational/confirmatory testing in the adjuvant setting. Nuvation Bio Inc. is listed as the sponsor for NCT07154706.

mechanism_context: Taletrectinib (also referenced as AB-106 / DS-6051b) is an oral, selective type I tyrosine kinase inhibitor targeting ROS1 and pan-NTRK (NTRK1/2/3). Preclinical enzymatic IC50 values reported are ROS1 0.207 nmol/L, NTRK1 0.622 nmol/L, NTRK2 2.28 nmol/L, and NTRK3 0.980 nmol/L, consistent with potent inhibition of ROS1 fusion-driven signaling and NTRK fusion signaling. ROS1 fusions are oncogenic drivers in a subset of NSCLC and other tumors; NTRK fusions similarly drive tumors in multiple histologies. Taletrectinib is positioned as a ROS1/NTRK TKI with activity against ROS1-driven disease and potential to address some resistance mutations.

trial_design: This is a Phase 3, multicenter, interventional, randomized, parallel-assignment, triple-masked (participant, care provider, investigator) double-blind study comparing taletrectinib oral capsules (400 mg once daily) with a matching placebo. The patient population includes individuals with ROS1-fusion positive NSCLC who have undergone complete tumor resection, specifically stages IB, II, and IIIA. The primary endpoint is Disease-free survival (DFS) by investigator assessment, defined as the time from randomization to disease recurrence or death (from any cause in the absence of recurrence), with a time frame of up to approximately 5 years after the first patient is randomized. Selected secondary endpoints include DFS rates at 2, 3, 4, and 5 years, Overall Survival (OS) with follow-up up to approximately 7 years, and incidence of adverse events (AEs) up to approximately 5 years. The estimated enrollment is 180 participants.

progress_timeline_date: DeLLphi-201 (Phase I, NCT03319940) had a data cutoff of July 19, 2022, showing an ORR of 23.4%, median DOR of 12.3 months, and median OS of 13.2 months. DeLLphi-301 (Phase 2, NCT05060016) is listed as a phase 2 study in relapsed/refractory SCLC. Multiple Phase 3 trials (DeLLphi-304, -305, -306, -312) and larger randomized studies (NCT07005128) are planned or underway, with Amgen citing DeLLphi-304 (randomized phase 3 monotherapy vs SOC in second-line) and DeLLphi-305 (tarlatamab+durvalumab vs durvalumab maintenance). A Lancet Oncology combination study reported enrollment and data between August 31, 2022, and January 30, 2024. Exact Phase 3 start and readout dates are company-controlled, but multiple trials are active or starting between 2023–2025 with readouts anticipated across 2024–2026.

trial_design: DeLLphi-201 (NCT03319940) is a Phase I dose-escalation and expansion study with IV dosing, evaluating safety (DLTs), objective response (modified RECIST), DOR, PFS, and OS. DeLLphi-301 (NCT05060016) is an open-label, single-arm Phase II study in 3L+ relapsed SCLC, focusing on efficacy, safety, and PK. NCT05361395 is a Phase 1b study assessing the safety and tolerability of tarlatamab with PD-L1 inhibitors +/- chemotherapy in first-line settings. NCT04885998 involves combination trials and PK evaluations. NCT07005128 is a randomized study comparing tarlatamab+durvalumab+carboplatin/etoposide versus durvalumab+carboplatin/etoposide, with primary endpoints likely including PFS/OS.

trial_design: The global Phase 3 trial (NCT07218146) for zocilurtatug pelitecan (ZL-1310) is designed as a randomized, open-label, parallel assignment study with a primary purpose of treatment. It aims to enroll approximately 665 patients across North America, Asia, and Europe. The patient population includes individuals with relapsed Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC) who have progressed on or after platinum-based first-line therapy as second-line therapy or after one line of chemotherapy followed by tarlatamab. The study includes three arms: Arm 1 and Arm 2 involve ZL-1310 as a single agent at Dose Level 1 and Dose Level 2, respectively. Arm 3 serves as an active comparator, utilizing Investigator's Choice of Therapy, which may include Tarlatamab, Topotecan, Lurbinectedin, or Amrubicin. Primary endpoints are Overall Response Rate (ORR) and Overall Survival (OS), while secondary endpoints include Duration of Response (DOR), Progression-Free Survival (PFS), and safety.

title: patritumab deruxtecan (MK-1022)
description: Patritumab deruxtecan (MK-1022) is an investigational HER3‑targeted antibody‑drug conjugate being tested in a Phase 3, randomized, double‑blind, placebo‑controlled trial (NCT07060807) for HER3‑positive metastatic breast cancer. The study enrolls first‑line patients, uses progression‑free survival as the primary endpoint, and is sponsored by Merck in partnership with Daiichi Sankyo.

While the breast cancer program is ongoing, there was a voluntary withdrawal of a biologics license application (BLA) for patritumab deruxtecan in previously treated EGFR-mutated NSCLC on May 29, 2025. Safety monitoring for interstitial lung disease (ILD)/pneumonitis is an important aspect of the development program, as these have been reported with DXd payload ADCs.

mechanism_context: Patritumab deruxtecan (also called HER3-DXd, MK-1022, U3-1402) is an investigational HER3-targeted antibody-drug conjugate (ADC). The molecule consists of a fully human anti-HER3 IgG1 antibody linked to a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (an exatecan derivative, “DXd”) via a tetrapeptide-based cleavable linker; ADC internalization and linker cleavage release the cytotoxic DXd payload into tumor cells.

trial_design: The NCT07060807 trial is a Phase 3, interventional, randomized, parallel assignment study with an open-label masking. The experimental arm involves participants receiving patritumab deruxtecan via intravenous (IV) infusion every 3 weeks (Q3W) for approximately 13 months. The active comparator arm involves Treatment of Physician's Choice (TPC), which can include paclitaxel, nab-paclitaxel, capecitabine, liposomal doxorubicin, or trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) for up to 13 months. The primary endpoint is Progression-free survival (PFS). The key patient population includes patients with metastatic HER3-positive breast cancer, consistent with HR+/HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer in the HERTHENA program.

mechanism_of_action: Patritumab deruxtecan is a HER3‑directed antibody‑drug conjugate composed of a fully human IgG1 anti‑HER3 monoclonal antibody linked via a cleavable tetrapeptide linker to a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (DXd, an exatecan derivative) with an approximate drug‑to‑antibody ratio of 8. Upon HER3 binding the ADC is internalized, the linker is cleaved in lysosomes, and the membrane‑permeable payload is released, causing DNA damage and a bystander antitumor effect.

trial_design: Trial name: IDeate‑Esophageal01 (ClinicalTrials.gov NCT06644781). Key design features: - Phase: 3, interventional, randomized, global, multicenter, open‑label. - Arms/comparator: Ifinatamab deruxtecan 12 mg/kg IV versus investigator’s (physician’s) choice of chemotherapy (paclitaxel, docetaxel or irinotecan hydrochloride). - Patient population: patients with unresectable advanced/metastatic ESCC with disease progression after platinum‑based chemotherapy and an immune checkpoint inhibitor; eligible patients must have received no more than one prior line of systemic therapy in the advanced/metastatic setting. - Primary endpoint: overall survival (OS). - Key secondary endpoints: progression‑free survival (PFS), objective response rate (ORR) assessed by blinded independent central review, and safety. - Planned enrollment: approximately 510 patients across Asia, Europe and North America. - Dose listed in press release: 12 mg/kg. - Biomarker selection: trial materials and the press release emphasize B7‑H3 as the molecular target and note B7‑H3 overexpression in many cancers (including ESCC) but do not state that enrollment requires B7‑H3 positivity or other biomarker selection criteria in the excerpts retrieved. (In other words, the documented eligibility in the press release refers to prior therapies and disease state rather than explicit B7‑H3 expression thresholds.)

target_population: Adults (≥18) with histologically or cytologically confirmed unresectable locally advanced or metastatic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC); disease progression after platinum‑based chemotherapy and an immune‑checkpoint inhibitor; at least one measurable lesion by RECIST v1.1; ECOG 0–1; adequate baseline tumor sample. mechanism_of_action: Ifinatamab deruxtecan (I‑DXd) is a B7‑H3‑directed antibody‑drug conjugate composed of an anti‑B7‑H3 IgG1 monoclonal antibody linked via a plasma‑stable, tumor‑selective cleavable tetrapeptide linker to a topoisomerase I inhibitor (DXd) payload, with an optimized drug‑to‑antibody ratio of ~4.
mechanism_of_action_basis: {"field":"mechanism_of_action","reasoning":"The peer‑reviewed article on PubMed (PMC12536792) describes the molecular structure and payload linkage of I‑DXd.","citations":[{"url":"https://synapse.patsnap.com/article/what-is-the-mechanism-of-action-of-ifinatamab-deruxtecan","title":"What is the mechanism of action of Ifinatamab deruxtecan?","excerpts":["The cytotoxic payload used in ifinatamab deruxtecan is a derivative of deruxtecan, a topoisomerase I inhibitor known for its ability to induce lethal DNA damage in rapidly dividing cells.","The antibody recognizes and binds to B7-H3 with high specificity, acting as a homing device to deliver the attached chemotherapeutic agent selectively to cancer cells.","The linker employed is designed to remain stable during systemic circulation, yet become selectively cleaved inside the target cell, ensuring that the potent cytotoxic agent is released only where it is needed.","Overview of Antibody–Drug Conjugates (ADCs)"]},{"url":"https://www.cancernetwork.com/view/phase-3-ideate-esophageal01-trial-initiated-for-unresectable-escc","title":"Fetched web page","excerpts":["Ifinatamab deruxtecan is specifically engineered as a potential B7-H3–directed DXd ADC. It was initially discovered by Daiichi Sankyo and is being jointly developed by Merck."]},{"url":"https://www.targetedonc.com/view/ifinatamab-deruxtecan-receives-fda-breakthrough-therapy-designation-in-sclc","title":"Ifinatamab Deruxtecan Receives FDA Breakthrough ...","excerpts":["Aug 18, 2025 — The mechanism of action of ifinatamab deruxtecan targets B7-H3, a protein that is overexpressed in a wide range of cancer types, including SCLC."]},{"url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12536792/","title":"IDeate-Lung02: a Phase 3 study of second-line ifinatamab ...","excerpts":["The mechanism of action of I-DXd provides the potential for a better clinical benefit-risk profile than that of traditional chemotherapy agents."]}],"confidence":"high"}

title: Ra223 and Enzalutamide Phase 3 Trial (NCT02194842)
description: The Phase 3 EORTC trial (NCT02194842) evaluates radium‑223 dichloride (Ra223) combined with enzalutamide in patients with asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) with bone‑only disease. The study randomizes 446 participants to Ra223 + enzalutamide versus enzalutamide alone. Primary endpoints are radiographic progression‑free survival (rPFS) by investigator assessment and centrally assessed progression‑free survival (PFS). Secondary endpoints include overall survival, time to first symptomatic skeletal event, pain assessments, and safety. An interim safety analysis showed reduced fracture rates with mandatory bone‑protecting agents. Enrollment began in October 2015, primary completion occurred in February 2024, and final study completion is projected for December 2028.

mechanism_context: Radium-223 (Ra-223; Xofigo) is an alpha-particle emitting radiopharmaceutical that preferentially localizes to areas of increased bone turnover (bone metastases) by forming complexes with bone mineral (hydroxyapatite). Its short-range, high-energy alpha emissions induce double-strand DNA breaks in adjacent tumor cells and bone marrow microenvironment, producing local cytotoxicity with limited systemic penetration. Enzalutamide (Xtandi) is a second-generation androgen receptor (AR) inhibitor that binds the AR and inhibits androgen binding, AR nuclear translocation, DNA binding and coactivator recruitment, thereby blocking AR-mediated transcription and tumor growth in prostate cancer.

progress_timeline_date: The study started in October 2015. Enrollment included 446 participants randomized 1:1, with the recruitment window from November 2015 to March 2023. Co-administration of bone-protecting agents was mandated as of March 2018. Primary completion was on 2024-02-19. The estimated study completion is December 2028. Key milestones include the publication of primary analysis (rPFS) in Annals of Oncology (2025) showing significant improvement with Ra-223, and subsequent overall survival analysis by EORTC confirming OS benefit. Interim safety analyses led to the bone-protecting agent mandate and showed reduced fracture rates when BPAs were used.

trial_design: This is a Phase 3, interventional, randomized, open-label, multicenter trial (EORTC-1333 / PEACE-3; ClinicalTrials.gov NCT02194842). The patient population consists of individuals with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), who are asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic (Brief Pain Inventory score <4), and have at least two bone metastases (bone-predominant disease). Patients were stratified by baseline pain, prior docetaxel, and bone-protecting agent use. The study arms are enzalutamide alone (160 mg daily) versus enzalutamide (160 mg daily) plus six cycles of Ra-223 (55 kBq/kg IV every 4 weeks). Randomization was 1:1. The primary endpoint is radiographic progression-free survival (rPFS) by investigator assessment. Key secondary endpoints include overall survival (OS), time to first symptomatic skeletal event (SSE), time to subsequent systemic treatment, pain progression, safety (adverse events, fractures), and quality of life measures. An interim safety analysis identified increased fractures without bone-protecting agents, leading to a mandate for co-administration of zoledronic acid or denosumab in 2018.

development_status: DATROWAY (datopotamab deruxtecan) has been approved in the U.S. for the treatment of adult patients with unresectable or metastatic HR-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer who have received prior endocrine-based therapy and chemotherapy. mechanism_context: DATROWAY (datopotamab deruxtecan) is a TROP2-directed DXd antibody-drug conjugate (ADC). It is comprised of a humanized anti-TROP2 IgG1 monoclonal antibody attached to multiple topoisomerase I inhibitor payloads (an exatecan derivative, DXd) via tetrapeptide-based cleavable linkers.

trial_design: TROPION-Breast01 is a global, randomized, multicenter, open-label Phase 3 study. It evaluates datopotamab deruxtecan versus investigator's choice of single-agent chemotherapy (eribulin, capecitabine, vinorelbine, or gemcitabine). The trial enrolled over 700 patients with inoperable or metastatic HR-positive, HER2-low or negative (IHC 0, IHC 1+ or IHC 2+/ISH-) breast cancer who have previously progressed on or are not suitable for endocrine therapy and have received at least one systemic therapy. The dual primary endpoints are progression-free survival (PFS) as assessed by blinded independent central review (BICR) and overall survival (OS). Key secondary endpoints include objective response rate (ORR), duration of response, investigator-assessed PFS, disease control rate, and time to first subsequent therapy.

current_standard_of_care: HR+/HER2‑negative metastatic breast cancer is typically treated first with endocrine therapy ± CDK4/6 inhibitor; upon progression, chemotherapy is used. Approved ADCs for this population include trastuzumab deruxtecan for HER2‑low disease and sacituzumab govitecan for HR+/HER2‑negative disease.

title: ziftomenib Phase 3 trial in frontline AML
description: Ziftomenib is a menin inhibitor being evaluated in a global Phase 3, randomized, double‑blind, placebo‑controlled trial (NCT07007312) for newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukaemia (AML) patients with NPM1‑mutated or KMT2A‑rearranged disease. The study enrolls first‑line patients, combines ziftomenib with standard intensive or non‑intensive chemotherapy, and is sponsored by Kura Oncology, Inc. The trial began dosing in September 2025 and aims to demonstrate efficacy and safety versus placebo + chemotherapy.

mechanism_context: Ziftomenib is an investigational oral menin inhibitor that disrupts the menin-dependent leukemogenic program (menin interaction with MLL/KMT2A), suppresses aberrant leukemogenic gene expression (eg, HOX/MEIS programs) and induces differentiation and anti-proliferative/apoptotic effects in NPM1-mutant and KMT2A-rearranged AML models. Preclinical and early clinical reports note differentiation induction and synergy with venetoclax. The biological rationale is that NPM1-mutant and KMT2A-rearranged AMLs are dependent on menin-mediated transcriptional programs; inhibiting menin is therefore expected to downregulate HOX/MEIS-driven leukemogenesis, providing a genotype-directed approach in these subtypes.

trial_design: This is a Phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial with two parallel substudies: a Nonintensive Therapy Study and an Intensive Therapy Study. In the Nonintensive Therapy Study, patients receive venetoclax + azacitidine (SOC) + ziftomenib versus venetoclax + azacitidine + placebo. In the Intensive Therapy Study, patients receive induction with cytarabine + daunorubicin (7+3) + ziftomenib versus 7+3 + placebo; consolidation with cytarabine + ziftomenib/placebo; and maintenance with ziftomenib/placebo. The patient population includes adults with newly diagnosed, untreated AML harboring NPM1-mutation or KMT2A-rearrangement. The nonintensive cohort is for older or medically unfit patients, while the intensive cohort is for medically fit patients. The trial is double-blinded, meaning neither the patient nor the physician knows if the patient is receiving ziftomenib or placebo. Primary endpoints for the Nonintensive Therapy Study (all countries) include Overall Survival (OS), and for US & US reference countries, Complete Remission (CR) rate per ELN 2022 criteria. Primary endpoints for the Intensive Therapy Study (all countries) include Event-free Survival (EFS), and for US & US reference countries, CR rate per ELN 2022 criteria with central bone marrow MRD negativity in NPM1-m patients. All primary endpoints are assessed up to 36 months after the last patient inclusion. Secondary endpoints typically include safety, response duration, MRD, relapse-free survival, and other efficacy measures, with a full list available in the ClinicalTrials.gov Study Plan.

target_population: Adults ≥18 years with untreated AML per WHO 2022 criteria, ECOG 0‑2, adequate organ function. Non‑intensive cohort requires documented NPM1 mutation and ineligibility for intensive therapy (age ≥75 or age <75 with ECOG 2 or organ impairment). Intensive cohort requires documented NPM1 mutation or KMT2A rearrangement (excluding PTD), FLT3 wild‑type or ITD ratio <0.05 or ineligibility for FLT3‑targeted therapy, LVEF ≥50%, and fitness for intensive therapy. Key exclusions include prior AML therapy (except hydroxyurea/leukapheresis), APL, blast‑phase CML, BCR‑ABL mutation, active CNS leukemia, and uncontrolled leukocytosis.

title: IMDELLTRA® (tarlatamab-dlle)
description: IMDELLTRA® (tarlatamab-dlle) is a first‑in‑class DLL3‑targeting bispecific T‑cell engager approved by the FDA in November 2025 for adult patients with extensive‑stage small‑cell lung cancer (ES‑SCLC) whose disease has progressed after platinum‑based chemotherapy. Approval is based on the global Phase 3 DeLLphi‑304 trial, which showed a 40 % reduction in risk of death and extended median overall survival to 13.6 months versus 8.3 months with standard chemotherapy.

trial_design: The DeLLphi-304 trial is a randomized, global Phase 3 study. It compared tarlatamab monotherapy against physician’s choice standard-of-care chemotherapy (including topotecan, lurbinectedin, or amrubicin) in adult patients with extensive-stage small-cell lung cancer (ES-SCLC) whose disease had progressed during or after platinum-based chemotherapy (second-line setting). The primary endpoint was Overall Survival (OS), with secondary endpoints including progression-free survival (PFS), patient-reported outcomes (PROs), and safety/tolerability. The primary analysis showed a substantial OS benefit with tarlatamab, reducing the risk of death by 40% and extending median OS to 13.6 months versus 8.3 months with standard chemotherapy. Improvements in PFS and favorable PROs and safety profile were also reported.

mechanism_context: ENHERTU (fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki; also called trastuzumab deruxtecan or T-DXd) is a HER2-directed antibody-drug conjugate (ADC). It combines a humanized anti-HER2 monoclonal antibody with a cleavable linker to a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (a derivative of exatecan). This ADC has a high drug-to-antibody ratio and is designed to bind HER2 on tumor cells, be internalized, release the cytotoxic payload, and exert both intracellular cytotoxicity and a bystander killing effect in tumors with low HER2 expression. This mechanism is relevant to HER2-low and HER2-ultralow breast cancer because the ADC can act even when HER2 expression is not high (IHC 1+, IHC 2+/ISH-, and IHC 0 with membrane staining in ultralow), thereby expanding HER2-directed therapy to populations historically considered HER2-negative.

trial_design: This is a Phase 3, randomized, open-label, global, interventional trial comparing trastuzumab deruxtecan (ENHERTU) monotherapy with investigator's choice chemotherapy. The patient population includes adults with hormone receptor (HR)-positive, HER2-low (IHC 1+ or IHC 2+/ISH-) or HER2-ultralow (IHC 0 with membrane staining) advanced or metastatic breast cancer whose disease progressed on endocrine therapy. Specific prior therapy requirements include progression on at least 2 prior lines of endocrine therapies for metastatic disease or progression within 6 months of starting first-line endocrine therapy combined with a CDK4/6 inhibitor. The study arms consist of ENHERTU monotherapy versus investigator's choice chemotherapy (standard-of-care single-agent chemotherapies). The primary endpoint is progression-free survival (PFS), with key secondary endpoints including overall survival (OS), objective response rate (ORR), duration of response (DoR), safety/tolerability, and patient-reported quality of life measures. The trial design also includes open-label and randomized allocation, with central testing for HER2 expression required.

development_status: Following the positive Phase 3 DeLLphi-304 results, tarlatamab-dlle (IMDELLTRA) has received regulatory approval. The FDA granted traditional/full approval for tarlatamab-dlle for the treatment of adult patients with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer (ES-SCLC) with disease progression on or after platinum-based chemotherapy, following a prior accelerated approval. The drug is now available for this labeled indication. The safety profile, as described in the prescribing information, includes treatment-emergent adverse events such as CRS, fatigue, decreased appetite, dysgeusia, pyrexia, constipation, musculoskeletal pain, nausea, and laboratory abnormalities like lymphopenia. The dose regimen in trials and the prescribing information specifies an initial 1 mg on Cycle 1 Day 1, followed by 10 mg on Days 8 and 15, and then every 2 weeks.

progress_timeline_date: Amgen announced positive Phase 3 DeLLphi-304 results on June 2, 2025, reporting a statistically significant and clinically meaningful overall survival (OS) benefit. The company stated that IMDELLTRA significantly reduced the risk of death by 40% and extended median overall survival by more than five months compared to chemotherapy. trial_design: The DeLLphi-304 study (NCT05740566) is a Phase 3, randomized, open-label study. It compares single-agent tarlatamab (Imdelltra) against standard-of-care chemotherapy (country-dependent options like topotecan, lurbinectedin, or amrubicin) with patients randomized 1:1. The patient population includes individuals with relapsed/recurrent extensive-stage SCLC after prior platinum-based first-line chemotherapy (second-line setting). Key stratification factors are prior anti-PD-1/PD-L1 exposure, chemotherapy-free interval, presence of brain metastases, and the specific SOC agent used. The primary endpoint is overall survival (OS), with key secondary endpoints including progression-free survival (PFS), objective response rate (ORR), duration of response, and safety.

title: Ipilimumab in Melanoma (NCT00324155)
description: Ipilimumab, a CTLA‑4 checkpoint inhibitor, was investigated in a Phase 3, randomized, double‑blind, placebo‑controlled trial (NCT00324155) for unresectable stage III/IV melanoma. The study enrolled approximately 400 patients, comparing ipilimumab 10 mg/kg every 3 weeks for four doses versus placebo, with overall survival as the primary endpoint and progression‑free survival, objective response rate, and safety as secondary endpoints. The trial met its primary endpoint, demonstrating a statistically significant improvement in overall survival. The sponsor was Bristol‑Myers Squibb; the trial received Breakthrough Therapy and Orphan Drug designations in the United States. The study began enrollment in 2008, completed recruitment in 2013, and final results were published in 2015.

development_status: The trial is completed, with study completion reported as 2013-10. The sponsor is Bristol-Myers Squibb. The trial and follow-up publications indicate that the trial met its primary endpoint, demonstrating a statistically significant overall survival benefit for ipilimumab + dacarbazine. Ipilimumab subsequently continued in development and is an approved CTLA-4 inhibitor for melanoma.

trial_design: Phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, two-arm study (CA184-024 / NCT00324155). Arms: dacarbazine + ipilimumab (ipilimumab 10 mg/kg q3w ×4) vs dacarbazine + placebo. Primary endpoint was overall survival (OS); secondary endpoints included progression-free survival (PFS), objective response rate (ORR), and safety.

title: Pembrolizumab (Keytruda®) – Phase 3 trial NCT03765918 in resectable locally advanced head and neck squamous‑cell carcinoma
description: Pembrolizumab (Keytruda®) is being evaluated in the Phase 3 trial NCT03765918, a randomized, double‑blind study of neoadjuvant and adjuvant pembrolizumab versus standard surgery ± radiation in patients with resectable locally advanced head and neck squamous‑cell carcinoma (HNSCC). The trial targets adult patients with stage III‑IVB HNSCC suitable for curative intent surgery, requires PD‑L1 expression ≥1 % for eligibility, and assesses pembrolizumab as monotherapy in both neoadjuvant and adjuvant settings.

trial_design: The trial, KEYNOTE-689 (NCT03765918), is a randomized, open-label, active-controlled Phase 3 study with parallel assignment. Patients were randomized 1:1 to receive either pembrolizumab + standard of care (SOC) or SOC alone. The population includes treatment-naïve patients with newly diagnosed, stage III or IVA resectable locally advanced HNSCC suitable for curative-intent surgery, with an ECOG performance status of 0–1. Exclusions included T4B, N3, distant metastases, non-specified head/neck primaries, and prior anti–PD-1/PD-L1 therapy. Efficacy outcomes were assessed by tumor PD-L1 combined positive score (CPS) subgroups (≥10, ≥1, and all participants). The experimental arm received neoadjuvant pembrolizumab 200 mg IV for 2 cycles (up to ~6 weeks) prior to surgery, followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab 200 mg IV for 15 cycles (up to ~45 weeks) plus investigator’s choice SOC adjuvant therapy (radiotherapy with or without cisplatin). The control arm received SOC (surgery ± adjuvant radiotherapy with or without cisplatin) without pembrolizumab. The primary endpoint is Event-free survival (EFS), evaluated sequentially in PD-L1 CPS ≥10, then CPS ≥1, then the overall population. Secondary endpoints include Overall survival (OS), major pathological response (mPR), pathological complete response (pCR), and safety/tolerability.

Its cooperative binding to PD-1 and VEGF enhances the blockade potency of both targets, providing a differentiated mechanism compared with single-target PD-1 or anti-VEGF agents.

trial_design: The HARMONi program encompasses a family of randomized Phase III studies in various non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) settings. HARMONi-3 is a randomized Phase III study in first-line metastatic NSCLC, comparing ivonescimab plus platinum-doublet chemotherapy against pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy. Primary endpoints for HARMONi-3 include overall survival (OS) and progression-free survival (PFS). Other HARMONi trials (e.g., HARMONi-A, HARMONi-2, HARMONi-6, HARMONi-7) evaluate ivonescimab in different lines of treatment, histologies (squamous vs. non-squamous), or PD-L1 strata, and against various PD-1/PD-L1 comparators or placebo plus chemotherapy.

trial_design: This is a Phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicenter global study (RUBY; ENGOT–EN6/GOG–3031). It is a two-part study: Part 1 compares dostarlimab + carboplatin–paclitaxel (6 cycles) followed by dostarlimab maintenance (1000 mg q6w up to 3 years) versus placebo + carboplatin–paclitaxel followed by placebo maintenance. Part 2 evaluates dostarlimab + carboplatin–paclitaxel followed by dostarlimab + niraparib versus placebo + carboplatin–paclitaxel followed by placebo. Randomization is 1:1 for Part 1, with parallel assignment and quadruple blinding (participant, care provider, investigator, outcomes assessor). The patient population includes adult female participants (>=18 years) with histologically/cytologically proven recurrent or primary advanced endometrial cancer (Stage III/IV or first recurrence) meeting specific inclusion criteria (ECOG 0–1, adequate organ function, etc.). Primary endpoints (dual primary) are Progression-free survival (PFS; investigator assessment and BICR) and Overall survival (OS), with time frames up to approximately 6 years. Key secondary endpoints include Objective response rate (ORR), duration of response (DOR), disease control rate (DCR), safety (AEs/SAEs), and patient-reported outcomes (EQ–5D–5L, EORTC QLQ–C30/Q LQ–EN24).

description: Bomedemstat (MK‑3543) is a lysine‑specific demethylase‑1 (LSD1) inhibitor being evaluated in a Phase 3 clinical trial (NCT06079879) for patients with myeloproliferative disorders. The study assesses efficacy and safety as monotherapy in a target population of adults with intermediate‑ or high‑risk disease who have failed prior therapy.

mechanism_context: Ifinatamab deruxtecan (I‑DXd, MK‑2400) is an investigational, B7‑H3–directed antibody–drug conjugate (ADC) built on Daiichi Sankyo’s DXd platform: a humanized anti‑B7‑H3 IgG1 monoclonal antibody conjugated via a cleavable tetrapeptide linker to a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (an exatecan‑derived DXd payload). B7‑H3 is reported to be overexpressed in multiple cancers including metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), supporting it as a therapeutic target.

Population: Patients with mCRPC after prior ARPI (per trial description/news); planned enrollment ~1,440 patients globally. Key endpoints: Co‑primary endpoints include overall survival (OS) and radiographic progression‑free survival (rPFS). Secondary endpoints include objective/response measures and safety. Randomization and comparator: Active comparator (docetaxel + prednisone); randomized design.

mechanism_of_action: Ifinatamab deruxtecan (I‑DXd) is a humanized anti‑B7‑H3 IgG1 antibody linked via a cleavable linker to a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (DXd). Binding to B7‑H3 on tumor cells leads to internalization and release of DXd, killing the cell and potentially exerting a by‑stander effect on neighboring B7‑H3‑negative cells.

mechanism_context: Epcoritamab (epcoritamab-bysp / EPKINLY) is a subcutaneously administered, bispecific CD3xCD20 T-cell–engaging antibody. It binds CD3 on T cells and CD20 on B cells to activate and redirect T cells to kill malignant CD20+ B cells, inducing T-cell–mediated cytotoxicity of B-cell malignancies. Clinical reports emphasize potent anti-tumor activity in B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma with an expected safety profile including cytokine release syndrome (generally low grade) and immune-mediated events. The biological context involves targeting CD20-positive B cells (including follicular lymphoma cells) and relying on the recruitment and activation of patient T cells. It is given subcutaneously to enable outpatient administration and is often evaluated in combination with anti-CD20 rituximab and immunomodulatory agent lenalidomide (R2) to potentially deepen responses.

trial_design: The official study title is "A Phase 3, Open-Label Study to Evaluate Safety and Efficacy of Epcoritamab in Combination With Rituximab and Lenalidomide (R2) Compared to R2 in Subjects With Relapsed or Refractory Follicular Lymphoma (EPCORE FL-1)." It is a Phase 3, randomized, open-label, multicenter, global trial. The experimental arm involves subcutaneous epcoritamab + rituximab + lenalidomide (epcoritamab + R2), while the control arm uses rituximab + lenalidomide (R2) alone. Key inclusion criteria include adults with histologically confirmed classic follicular lymphoma (grades 1–3a), stage II–IV, CD20+ tumor, who are relapsed or refractory after at least one prior line of anti-lymphoma therapy. The primary efficacy endpoint is progression-free survival (PFS) by blinded independent central review. Secondary endpoints include overall response rate (ORR), complete response (CR) or complete metabolic response (CMR) rates, overall survival (OS), duration of response (DOR), safety/tolerability, and exploratory pharmacodynamic/pharmacokinetic assessments.

development_status: Belantamab mafodotin was initially FDA‑approved (accelerated) in 2020 for heavily pretreated RRMM but the U.S. approval was withdrawn following negative phase 3 data (DREAMM‑3) reported in 2022–2023. However, combination trials (including DREAMM‑7 and DREAMM‑8) subsequently reported positive results (statistically significant and clinically meaningful PFS benefit for BPd vs PVd in DREAMM‑8 as reported at major conferences in 2024 and further publications in 2025). These positive combination results prompted renewed regulatory activity and region-specific approvals/reviews in 2024–2025. The agent’s development has therefore transitioned from a withdrawn monotherapy indication toward development and regulatory review in combination regimens.

mechanism_context: Belantamab mafodotin (Blenrep) is an antibody–drug conjugate (ADC) that targets B‑cell maturation antigen (BCMA) on plasma cells. The ADC delivers a cytotoxic microtubule-disrupting payload (monomethyl auristatin F, MMAF) into BCMA-expressing myeloma cells; cell killing occurs via internalization and intracellular release of the toxin and also through Fc-mediated immune effector functions (ADCC/ADCP). BCMA is highly expressed on late-stage B cells and malignant plasma cells in multiple myeloma, making it a validated antigen for targeted therapies. Ocular toxicity (corneal epithelial events) is a characteristic and dose-limiting toxicity of the mafodotin ADC class and has been an important safety consideration and dose-modification strategy in belantamab trials.

progress_timeline_date: The trial identifier is NCT04484623 (DREAMM-8). Enrollment began around 2021, with some summaries listing July 2021 as an early listing date. Key milestones include the reporting of DREAMM-8 results in major conference abstracts (ASCO/JCO/ASCO presentations) in 2024, showing a progression-free survival (PFS) benefit for BPd vs PVd. Patient-reported outcomes were published in Lancet Haematol in November 2025. Belantamab mafodotin received accelerated regulatory recognition (FDA approval in 2020), was withdrawn from the US market after failure to meet endpoints in DREAMM-3 (withdrawal action in 2022), and later combination-trial data (including DREAMM-7/DREAMM-8) produced positive results leading to renewed regulatory interest and regional approvals/reviews in 2024–2025. The ClinicalTrials.gov entry for NCT04484623 serves as the authoritative source for actual/estimated study start, primary completion, and study completion dates.

trial_design: DREAMM-8 (NCT04484623) is a randomized, open-label, phase 3 trial comparing belantamab mafodotin + pomalidomide + dexamethasone (BPd) versus bortezomib + pomalidomide + dexamethasone (PVd) in patients with relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma. The patient population includes RRMM patients with at least one prior line of therapy. The study arms are Arm A: belantamab mafodotin + pomalidomide + dexamethasone (BPd) and a comparator arm: bortezomib + pomalidomide + dexamethasone (PVd). The primary efficacy endpoint reported was progression-free survival (PFS). Secondary endpoints included overall response rate (ORR), overall survival (OS), safety/tolerability (including ocular events), and patient‑reported outcomes.

mechanism_of_action: Belantamab mafodotin (belamaf) is an afucosylated anti‑BCMA IgG1 antibody‑drug conjugate linked via a non‑cleavable maleimidocaproyl (MC) linker to monomethyl auristatin F (MMAF). It binds BCMA on myeloma cells, is internalized, and the MC linker releases MMAF in lysosomes, disrupting microtubules, causing G2/M arrest and apoptosis. Afucosylation enhances FcγRIIIa engagement, augmenting ADCC and ADCP.

mechanism_context: Pembrolizumab (KEYTRUDA) is an anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody that blocks PD-1 interaction with PD-L1/PD-L2, thereby reactivating T cells and antitumor immune responses. This immunologic mechanism is relevant in muscle-invasive bladder cancer (MIBC), a molecularly diverse disease, where immune checkpoint blockade can restore anti-tumor immunity in tumors that express PD-1/PD-L1.

trial_design: This is a global, randomized phase III perioperative study (KEYNOTE-905 / MK-3475-905) comparing perioperative pembrolizumab plus radical cystectomy with pelvic lymph node dissection (RC+PLND) versus RC+PLND alone in cisplatin-ineligible muscle-invasive bladder cancer (MIBC) patients. Some sources indicate additional experimental arms that include enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab + surgery. Example arm descriptions include: Pembrolizumab + Surgery (Experimental), where participants receive 3 preoperative cycles of pembrolizumab, followed by standard of care surgery, followed by 14 cycles of postoperative pembrolizumab (each cycle is 21 days); Surgery alone (Active Comparator), which involves standard of care RC+PLND; and an Enfortumab Vedotin + Pembrolizumab + Surgery (Experimental) arm, involving a preoperative combination followed by a postoperative combination and then pembrolizumab monotherapy cycles. The patient population consists of cisplatin-ineligible patients with histologically confirmed urothelial carcinoma staged as muscle-invasive (e.g., cT2-T4aN0M0 or certain node-positive configurations per protocol), with predominant (≥50%) urothelial histology and planned radical cystectomy with pelvic lymph node dissection. Primary and secondary endpoints are listed on the ClinicalTrials.gov study record, with the primary completion date having been reached on 2025-06-06. Known identifiers include MK-3475-905 and KEYNOTE-905, along with other registry identifiers such as EV-303, EudraCT, and jRCT numbers.

trial_design: The DREAMM-7 trial (NCT04246047) is a Phase 3, randomized, open-label study sponsored by GlaxoSmithKline. Patients were randomized 1:1 to receive either belantamab mafodotin + bortezomib + dexamethasone (BVd) or daratumumab + bortezomib + dexamethasone (DVd). The study population included adults with relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma who had received prior therapy, excluding those refractory or intolerant to daratumumab or bortezomib, or with prior BCMA-directed therapy. Efficacy endpoints for regulatory evaluation included progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS). The median PFS was 31.3 months in the BVd arm versus 10.4 months in the DVd arm (HR 0.31). The median OS was not reached in the BVd arm versus 35.7 months in the DVd arm (HR 0.49).

development_status: Dostarlimab (Jemperli) is an approved anti-PD-1 agent with FDA approvals for dMMR solid tumors and certain endometrial cancer indications. It is part of GSK's expanded immuno-oncology program (AZUR program) for dMMR/MSI-H colorectal cancer and other tumor types. The FDA has granted accelerated approval for dostarlimab-gxly in dMMR advanced solid tumors based on the GARNET trial and approved a companion diagnostic. GSK has also obtained Breakthrough Therapy Designation for locally advanced dMMR/MSI-H rectal cancer. The NCT05855200 trial is an active GSK Phase 3 perioperative study, with its record last updated on 2025-08-08, indicating ongoing development.

trial_design: The trial NCT05855200 is a Phase 3, randomized, parallel-assignment, open-label interventional study with a primary purpose of treatment. It compares perioperative dostarlimab (pre- and post-surgery) against a standard-of-care perioperative strategy (FOLFOX or CAPEOX) or observation post-surgery. The target population is untreated T4N0 or stage III dMMR/MSI-H resectable colon cancer. Specific primary and secondary endpoints were not explicitly captured in the provided verbatim excerpts, but are typically present on the ClinicalTrials.gov page.

mechanism_context: Niraparib is a PARP inhibitor that targets single-strand DNA break repair, exploiting homologous recombination deficiency in tumors. Pembrolizumab is an anti-PD-1 immune checkpoint inhibitor that reactivates anti-tumor T-cell responses. The combination is rationalized by preclinical and early clinical data suggesting PARP inhibitors can increase tumor DNA damage/neoantigen load and activate innate immune signaling pathways, potentially sensitizing tumors to PD-1 blockade. Phase-2 clinical data of niraparib + pembrolizumab in advanced NSCLC showed clinical activity, supporting its evaluation in the randomized phase-3 ZEAL-1L study.

trial_design: This is a Phase 3, multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel-assignment, triple-masked study. The experimental arm involves niraparib plus pembrolizumab, while the comparator arm uses matching placebo plus pembrolizumab. The primary hypotheses focus on the benefit of niraparib plus pembrolizumab versus placebo plus pembrolizumab for Progression-Free Survival (PFS) and Overall Survival (OS). The study population includes participants with stage IIIB/IIIC or IV NSCLC who achieved stable disease, partial response, or complete response after standard-of-care first-line platinum-based induction chemotherapy with pembrolizumab. A total of 666 participants were enrolled.

trial_design: This is a Phase 3, randomized, open-label, parallel-assignment study with 1:1 randomization, primarily for treatment. The experimental arm receives SKB264 (sac-TMT) on Days 1, 15, and 29 of each 6-week cycle plus pembrolizumab on Day 1 of each 6-week cycle. The comparator arm receives pembrolizumab monotherapy on Day 1 of each 6-week cycle. Key eligibility criteria include adults aged 18-75 years with histologically/cytologically confirmed stage IIIB/IIIC or IV NSCLC not amenable to radical therapy, PD-L1 tumor proportion score (TPS) ≥1%, ECOG 0-1, measurable disease per RECIST 1.1, and no prior systemic therapy in the locally advanced/metastatic setting. The primary endpoint is progression-free survival (PFS), with secondary endpoints typically including overall survival (OS), objective response rate (ORR), duration of response (DoR), and safety.

description: Imdelltra is a DLL3‑targeting T‑cell engager evaluated in a Phase 3 trial (NCT06117774) for maintenance therapy after first‑line treatment of limited‑stage small‑cell lung cancer (LS‑SCLC). mechanism_context: Tarlatamab (marketed as Imdelltra) is a DLL3-targeted bispecific T-cell engager. It functions by binding to DLL3 on tumor cells and CD3 on T cells, thereby redirecting T cells to mediate the lysis of tumor cells. DLL3 is specifically expressed on small-cell lung cancer (SCLC) cells, making it a relevant therapeutic target in this cancer type.

trial_design: DeLLphi-306 is a Phase 3, randomized (1:1), double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicenter, parallel-group study. It enrolls approximately 400 adult participants (aged ≥18) with histologically/cytologically confirmed limited-stage small-cell lung cancer (LS-SCLC) who have completed concurrent chemoradiation without progression (CR/PR/SD) and have an ECOG Performance Status of 0 or 1. Patients with extensive-stage SCLC, interstitial lung disease, prior DLL3-pathway inhibitors, or recent major surgery are typically excluded. The experimental arm receives intravenous tarlatamab (AMG 757) on Cycle 1 Day 1, 8, and 15, then every 2 weeks (Q2W) thereafter, with each cycle lasting 28 days. The comparator arm receives placebo intravenously on the same schedule. The primary endpoint is progression-free survival (PFS) assessed by blinded independent central review (BICR) per RECIST 1.1. Key secondary endpoints include overall survival (OS), investigator-assessed PFS, objective response rate, disease control rate, duration of response, time to progression, pharmacokinetics, immunogenicity, and safety.

title: LYNPARZA™ (MK-7339)
description: LYNPARZA™ (olaparib, MK‑7339) is a poly (ADP‑ribose) polymerase (PARP) inhibitor being tested in a Phase 3 randomized, double‑blind, placebo‑controlled trial (NCT04380636) for patients with advanced non‑small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). The study enrolls adult participants who have progressed after prior platinum‑based chemotherapy, requires measurable disease, and mandates tumor testing for homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) or specific BRCA1/2 mutations. The primary endpoint is overall survival; secondary endpoints include progression‑free survival, objective response rate, and safety. The trial is sponsored by Merck in collaboration with AstraZeneca and holds Fast Track and Orphan Drug designations in the United States.

mechanism_context: LYNPARZA (olaparib, MK-7339) is a PARP inhibitor. PARP inhibitors block poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase-mediated repair of single-strand DNA breaks, leading to accumulation of DNA damage and tumor cell death, particularly in tumors with homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) such as BRCA1/2-mutant cancers. The KEYLYNK-012 program investigates whether adding olaparib to anti-PD-1 therapy after concurrent chemoradiation (CCRT) can improve outcomes in unresectable, locally advanced stage III NSCLC by leveraging DNA damage and immunomodulatory effects of PARP inhibition. Olaparib is approved for use in advanced ovarian, breast, pancreatic, and prostate cancer.

trial_design: This is a Phase 3, interventional, multicenter, randomized, placebo- and active-controlled, double-blind study (blinded between olaparib vs placebo within pembrolizumab arms). Participants are randomized 1:1:1 into three groups: Group A receives pembrolizumab + CCRT followed by pembrolizumab + placebo; Group B receives pembrolizumab + CCRT followed by pembrolizumab + olaparib; and Group C receives CCRT followed by durvalumab (active comparator). Key eligibility criteria include unresectable, locally advanced stage III NSCLC planned for definitive CCRT. The trial's rationale involves DNA damage/HRD biology, with biomarker testing for PARP sensitivity. Primary and secondary endpoints listed on ClinicalTrials.gov include incidence of adverse events, discontinuation rate due to AE, objective response rate (ORR) per RECIST 1.1 by BICR, and change from baseline in role functioning using EORTC QLQ-C30.

development_status: The trial (INTerpath-001) is an active, ongoing Phase 3 study, as registered on clinicaltrials.gov. The sponsor is Merck Sharp & Dohme LLC, with ModernaTX, Inc. as a collaborator. The initiation of this Phase 3 study follows earlier clinical results evaluating mRNA-4157/V940 plus pembrolizumab, which showed evidence of improved recurrence-free survival and distant-metastasis-free survival. Corporate press materials have reported multi-year (3-year) data indicating sustained improvement in recurrence-free survival and distant-metastasis-free survival for the combination compared to pembrolizumab alone, supporting its continued development into this Phase 3 adjuvant program.

drug_indication: The drug is Intismeran autogene (V940), also known as mRNA-4157, an individualized neoantigen therapy. It is being studied for the adjuvant treatment of participants with high-risk, resected Stage II-IV melanoma to prevent recurrence after surgical resection. The trial compares V940 plus pembrolizumab versus placebo plus pembrolizumab.

mechanism_context: Intismeran autogene (V940; formerly mRNA-4157) is an individualized neoantigen therapy, an mRNA-based vaccine tailored to a patient’s tumor mutanome and HLA type. It encodes up to approximately 34 patient-specific tumor neoantigens in an mRNA construct delivered in a lipid nanoparticle formulation. The mRNA is translated in host cells, and the encoded neoantigen peptides enter endogenous antigen-processing and presentation pathways, provoking de novo and/or boosted tumor-specific CD8+ and CD4+ T-cell responses. The therapy is administered with pembrolizumab (an anti-PD-1 agent) because the vaccine increases neoantigen-specific T-cell responses while PD-1 blockade reduces inhibitory signaling, thereby enhancing antitumor immunity.

Neoantigens derived via the internal neoantigen selection algorithm (appendix p 13) and encoded in mRNA-4157 can be endogenously translated to enter the natural cellular antigen processing and presentation pathways. Subsequently, mRNA-4157 can stimulate reactive T cells targeting patient-specific tumour neoantigens, representing a novel modality for the treatment of cancer. trial_design: The trial, NCT05933577 (INTerpath-001), is a Phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo- and active-comparator-controlled study. It involves two arms: an experimental arm receiving Intismeran autogene (IM) plus Pembrolizumab (IV), and an active comparator arm receiving Placebo plus Pembrolizumab. Intismeran autogene is administered via intramuscular injection for up to 9 doses every 3 weeks, while pembrolizumab is given intravenously for up to 9 doses every 6 weeks. Treatment continues until recurrence, unacceptable toxicity, or for a total duration of up to approximately 56 weeks. The study uses a parallel assignment model with triple masking (Participant, Investigator, Outcomes Assessor), described as double-blind. The primary endpoint is Distant Metastasis-Free Survival (DMFS), defined as the time from study start to cancer spread or death from any cause, with a timeframe of up to approximately 85 months. Key secondary endpoints include Overall Survival (OS) (up to approximately 85 months), safety endpoints (number of participants with adverse events and discontinuations due to AEs over approximately 56-68 weeks), and quality-of-life change assessed by the EORTC QLQ-C30 global health/QoL score. The patient population includes adults (18 years and older) of all sexes with high-risk Stage II-IV melanoma in the adjuvant setting. Exclusions include other known cancers that have metastasized or required treatment within the past 3 years, severe reactions to study medications, or unrecovered major surgery/ongoing surgical complications. The estimated enrollment is 1,089 participants.

mechanism_of_action: Intismeran autogene (V940) is an individualized mRNA‑based neoantigen vaccine that encodes up to 34 patient‑specific neoantigens in a lipid‑nanoparticle formulation. After intramuscular injection the mRNA is translated in vivo, the neoantigen peptides are presented on HLA molecules, and they stimulate de‑novo and existing T‑cell responses against tumor‑specific antigens, enhancing the activity of checkpoint inhibition.

{"field":"mechanism_of_action","reasoning":"The ScienceDirect article describes the composition and hypothesized immune mechanism of mRNA‑4157 (V940).","citations":[{"url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12474233/","title":"Individualized mRNA Vaccines in Melanoma—Where Do We ...","excerpts":["Building an individualized mRNA vaccine follows four main steps:

1\. NGS of both neoplastic tissue and peripheral blood specimens of the patient: NGS has revolutionized cancer genomics during the last decades [ [70]() ], allowing parallel analysis of large cancer and normal cell DNA sequences, in a fast and efficient manner. Cancer and normal genome sequences may then be compared by a computational system capable of spotting alterations, patterns, and motives, and that is highly suspicious of deriving from somatic mutations taking place within tumor cells [ [71]() , [72]() ]. In this way, antigens unique to each patient and host may be identified and used as a boost of innate immunity. 2\. Selection of the most immunogenic neoantigens: The characteristic cancer neoantigens can be evaluated for their potential to form stable connections with HLA proteins and TCRs, in order to reassure optimal antigen presentation and T lymphocyte activation, respectively.
